column2: The 1980 XIII Olympic Winter Games at Lake Placid, New York will include construction or upgrade of major venues such as a new Field House/Arena, a refrigerated 400-meter Speed Skating Oval at the high school, 70 and 90 Meter Ski Jumps at Intervale, refrigerated bobsled and luge runs at Mt. Van Hoevenberg, expanded Alpine facilities at Whiteface Mountain, an Olympic Village at Ray Brook (designed for after-use as a federal correctional institution), and a Television Center on the Chubb River. The program is supported by a roughly $50 million LPOOC construction budget plus about $9.3 million i…

column2: The directly affected communities are the Town of North Elba (including the Village of Lake Placid), the Village of Saranac Lake, and the Town of Wilmington, with secondary effects in surrounding Essex and Franklin County towns such as Keene, Jay, Harrietstown, Elizabethtown, and others. The area has a permanent population of roughly 6,500–12,000 that swells with seasonal residents and tourists, and faces challenges including high seasonal unemployment, incomes well below the state average, nearly 11% of families in poverty, and overcrowded or substandard housing in some towns. Specific neigh…

The EIS evaluated a No Olympics alternative, alternative sites for each major facility, alternative distribution patterns concentrating or dispersing venues, and growth scenarios ranging from a no-Olympics baseline through low-, mid-, and high-range projections. Site alternatives examined included Bassett Mountain and Mt. Whitney for the 90 Meter Ski Jump; the Horse Show Grounds, Fish & Game Club, Holiday Inn, and existing Ray Brook complex for the Field House, Speed Skating Oval, and Olympic Village; and the Airport/Horse Show Grounds for the Television Center to avoid the Chubb River floodp…

Construction will cause short-term soil disturbance, erosion, dust, noise, and tree clearing—including roughly 57 acres of forest at Ray Brook, about 10 acres at Whiteface, and filling of about 3,000 square feet of the Ausable River floodplain at the 90 Meter Ski Jump—with potential siltation of the Chubb, Ausable, and Ray Brook waterways. The 266-foot ski jump tower at Intervale will create a significant long-term visual impact on the rural setting of John Brown's Farm and on travel corridors along Routes 73 and 86, while the refrigerated venues, Olympic Village, and Television Center will c…

A North Elba town referendum approved the Olympic concept, and household surveys showed strong satisfaction with local police and fire services, but residents and officials voiced concerns about fiscal burdens, with the LPOOC affirming the Games will not proceed if they impose severe local costs. The 90 Meter ski jump's height drew particular controversy, with the Adirondack Park Agency granting conceptual approval at Intervale while NYSDEC Commissioner Peter A.A. Berle voted to disapprove, and the State Historic Preservation Officer found no adverse effect on John Brown's Farm. Extensive con…
